How I Study: A Visual Data Story of My Study Habits

What did I study?

Over the semester, I logged my study sessions using a Google Form, recording the course, time of day, and how long each session lasted. I collected 55 observations across four courses: Stats 220, Stats 330, Phil 360, and Phil 327.

The chart below shows the total time I spent studying for each course. Stats 220 and Stats 330 dominated my workload, while the two philosophy papers required far less time.

Bar chart showing total study time per course, with Stats 220 having the most at around 900 minutes and Phil 327 the least at around 110 minutes

When did I study best?

I was curious whether my session lengths varied depending on the time of day. The plot below shows each individual study session as a point, with boxplots summarising the distribution for each time period.

Morning and afternoon sessions had a similar spread, while evening sessions tended to be slightly shorter on average. Sessions longer than 30 minutes were more common in the afternoon.

Boxplot with jittered points showing session duration by time of day, with points coloured by whether the session was short or long

How did my study change over time?

The line chart below shows how many study sessions I completed each week. There was a clear dip around early April mainly because I finished a few assignments around that time. Study intensity then ramped back up through April and peaked in early May as new assignment deadlines approached.

Line chart showing weekly number of study sessions over time, with a dip in early April and a peak in early May

What did I learn?

My data reveals that I tend to spread my study across the day fairly evenly, but my overall study effort is heavily weighted towards my statistics courses. The time-based pattern shows I respond to upcoming deadlines rather than maintaining a steady pace throughout the semester.
